title: The Development of Cartography from the Age of Exploration to Modern Techniques

lesson.learn: The early maps created during the Age of Exploration were often inaccurate due to the limited understanding of geography, cartography, and navigation techniques. The lack of precise measurement tools and instruments, coupled with the reliance on hearsay and myths, led to significant errors, including misplaced continents, distorted coastlines, and fictional waterways. This resulted in a multitude of errors that persisted for centuries.
lesson.simpler: During the Age of Exploration, mapmakers were limited by the technological capabilities of their time. They lacked access to precise measurement tools, such as the sextant, and relied on estimates and rough calculations. This led to inaccuracies in their maps.
lesson.keyTakeaway: The accuracy of early maps was compromised due to limited knowledge and inaccurate methods. The introduction of more advanced navigation tools and scientific methods gradually improved the accuracy of maps.
lesson.deeperDive: A notable example of an early map with significant errors is the Waldseemüller Map, created by Martin Waldseemüller in 1507. This map placed the New World (the Americas) west of Asia and Europe, challenging the prevailing geocentric model. The map's geographical inaccuracies were likely due to the limited availability of reliable geographic data and the use of outdated navigational methods.
lesson.funFact: The development of cartography was also influenced by the availability of geographic data. For instance, the Portuguese established a network of coastal fortifications in the 16th century, which enabled the creation of more accurate maps of the coastline.

places: The Bodleian Library at Oxford University
years: 1569: Gerardus Mercator publishes his famous world map
1571: The Treaty of Tordesillas establishes Spanish and Portuguese claims to the New World
1800s: Cartography begins to adopt more accurate techniques, such as triangulation
concepts: Scientific cartography (use of satellites and aerial photography)
